data.title: Trump Weighs New Tariffs And Auto Sector Relief
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Uncertainty lingers

Market participants remain wary of President Donald Trump's trade policies, with latest updates indicating more tariffs—and rollbacks—may be on the way. Pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports are under scrutiny for potential tariffs, while the automobile sector may be offered some respite in the days ahead.

Review underway: On April 1, the Trump administration began investigating the impact of pharmaceutical and semiconductor imports on national security, likely setting the stage for tariffs on the sectors. The Department of Commerce's pharma investigation covers generic and non-generic drugs, active pharma ingredients, and derivative products. The semiconductor probe covers items including leading-edge chips, chipmaking equipment, silicon wafers, and "downstream products that contain semiconductors."

The probes will look into current and projected demand for the pharma and semiconductor products, the ability of domestic production to meet demand, and the potential for export restrictions. The chip probe will also assess "the ability of foreign nations to weaponize their control over semiconductors and SME supply chains." The investigations were initiated under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962, which allows the president to adjust imports that are found to threaten national security.

Tariff relief? In other news, Trump floated a potential reprieve for the 25% tariffs on automobile and auto part imports, given the industry's deeply integrated North American supply chains. "I'm looking at something to help some car companies with it," Trump told reporters. "They're switching to parts that were made in Canada, Mexico and other places… they need a little bit of time because they're going to make them here." Trump's signal was enough to lift top automakers' shares – GM, F, STLA – which ended on a higher note on Monday.

What else is happening...

WSB survey results: Better trade deals for the U.S. are expected.

Yellen: Bond selloff signals 'loss of confidence' in policymaking.

Consumer polls - Mixed inflation outlook, confidence worsens.

Trump administration to end rule capping credit card late fee.

Lawmakers call on White House to get rid of AI Diffusion rule.

Palantir-NATO deal places it in 'prime position' for AI in defense.

Bristol Myers' (BMY) heart disease drug fails in late-stage trial.

Wall Street-backed crypto bank Anchorage under investigation.

Air travel slumps: U.S. airports see noticeable decline in traffic.

Over $2B of Harvard University's federal funding gets blocked.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan +0.8%. Hong Kong +0.2%. China +0.2%. India +2.1%.
In Europe, at midday, London +1%. Paris +0.5%. Frankfurt +1.5%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow +0.2%. S&P +0.2%. Nasdaq +0.3%. Crude -0.6% to $61.17. Gold +0.5% to $3,241.30. Bitcoin +1.3% to $85,757.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +2 bps to 4.39%.

Today's Economic Calendar

08:30 AM Empire State Mfg Survey
08:30 AM Import/Export Prices
11:35 AM Fed's Barkin Speech
07:10 PM Fed's Cook Speech

Companies reporting earnings today »

data.title: trivago: I Think It's Dead Money
data.description:
As tourism normalizes and returns to pre-pandemic levels, many companies see rebounding revenues and margins. Being an online search platform, trivago N.V. (NASDAQ:TRVG) receives positive spillovers of increased travel demand with improved topline performance. However, I have concerns about its business model, which can limit its growth potential. Technical risks are also evident.

trivago’s Decent Closing of 2024

As 2024 closed, trivago N.V. saw the positive impact of improving travel trends and its efforts to rejuvenate or reestablish its brand. This was reflected in its decent performance during Q4 2024. Its operating revenue amounted to €94.8M, a 3.3% YoY growth from €91.7M. This far exceeded its performance for the whole year despite seasonality. It is also worth noting that it appears to be rebounding if we trace the YoY change in its quarterly revenue from 2023. Referral revenue remained its primary revenue component, with growth driven by the Americas and other unspecified regions.

Some contributors were the gradual economic recovery despite the sudden inflation uptick in Q4 2024, decreasing oil and fuel prices, and increased internet users. With trivago’s popularity enhanced by its ties with Expedia, customer loyalty also helped.

Another impressive thing it showed during the quarter was its operational efficiency despite the sticky inflation. As we can see, costs and expenses remained relatively steady. As a result, its operating margin rose from 4.8% to 8.4% YoY.

Drivers

These are some things I believe may help trivago in improving its performance.

Improving Liquidity

One thing I easily noticed is its increasing cash reserves throughout the year, which can indicate increasing cash returns from its operations. True enough, its FCF has improved a lot in the past seven quarters.

Moreover, cash alone is enough to cover all its liabilities in a single payment, making it very liquid. It also has no existing debt. This positions TRVG well amid macroeconomic volatility. This can also be an assurance of its sustainability regardless of its performance.

However, there are some concerns about its Balance Sheet. One is that its book value appears to be shrinking. Its PP&E and intangibles continue to drop, suggesting continued depreciation and that TRVG is slow to replenish or replace its equipment with new ones. While I understand that its business nature is not asset-heavy, it must ensure sufficient and updated equipment and software amid the tighter competition and the AI revolution.

Decreasing Oil Prices

Tourism-related businesses see more glimmers of hope amid the declining price of Brent and WTI. Now, Brent is only $63.7 per barrel, while WTI is $60.5 per barrel. Both are already more than 30% lower than their peak price in 2024. In addition, they are also about half their value during 2022 highs. And I think this can be sustained by the planned OPEC supply hike in the next two months. Increased oil output can further lower global oil and fuel prices.

If this continues, transportation expenses and airfare may be positively affected. This can improve travel spending and demand this year. This can also help stabilize inflation as lower oil prices will affect many industries, especially those using oil and fuel. Aside from airlines and vehicles, lower oil and fuel prices can help restaurants, logistics, shipping, and manufacturing stabilize their costs. If not, it can at least cushion the impact of tariffs on inflation and consumer confidence.

Travel Resilience

In the US alone, travel trends are still showing an optimistic outlook this year. In a study, 92% of Americans may travel this year with a budget nearly double the average amount in 2024. What’s more interesting is that we may expect 56% of Americans to travel more this Spring.

This can be supported by some factors, including decreasing oil and fuel prices and decreased inflation last March. Additionally, hybrid and/or remote work setups allow many people to travel anytime and anywhere they want.

Laggards

These are some of my concerns about trivago.

Business Model

I like its business model for having manageable cost sensitivity and capital intensity. Inflationary headwinds do not necessarily affect their operating cost and expenses.

However, this also poses risks to trivago. One is that its business model is easy to emulate.

In addition, I think it’s lagging behind the market in terms of product or digital innovation as it has only launched its AI Smart Search and personalization recently. And I believe its shrinking fixed assets and intangibles are contributors. Another concern is that it has become easier to market or advertise on social media, which can lower its traffic in the long run.

Increased Competition

Since its business model is easy to establish, we can expect its market space to become oversaturated or crowded. Almost everyone from various sites and apps like Airbnb and even Google competes for the same users.

This can affect its revenue. Although it earns by clicks, not conversions, risks must not be discounted. If these clicks do not result in confirmed bookings, advertiser bids may decrease or even be pulled out. In the long run, the value per click may become eroded, making it hard for TRVG to justify ad spending.

One threat to watch out for is the increasing number of advertisers and evolving ad formats, which can affect the traffic and marketability of advertisements. We can already see its weak performance in its core market, Developed Europe.

Hotels and Airlines as Indirect Competitors

Today, many airlines and hotels allow direct booking on their websites and apps. And as they become more accessible online, many travelers now do direct bookings. This lowers TRVG’s value propositions as direct bookings remove the need for some of its features like price comparisons.

Note that many hotels and airlines promote direct bookings through exclusive rewards like discounts and points. Popular brands like Marriott International, Inc. (MAR), Hilton Worldwide Holdings Inc. (HLT), and InterContinental Hotels Group PLC (IHG) have been leading this trend since the pandemic. After all, this reduces their advertising expense as travelers won’t have to click trivago ads. In addition, many hotels aim to increase customer loyalty.

To conclude this part, I’m not saying all aspects mentioned will lead to trivago’s bankruptcy. To be more realistic, I just believe these risks can shrink TRVG’s value and market share in the long run. As more competitors enter and as direct bookings increase, advertising bids may decrease.

Price Ratio

TRVG has decreased dramatically in the past five years. Yet, I think that’s justifiable considering its key metrics, particularly its book value. As we can see, its price has generally moved along with BVPS. If we try to do a regression analysis from its IPO, we can see a 72% correlation. This shows that a change in BVPS will lead to a 70% chance of the price moving in the same direction. Meanwhile, its p-value of 0.04 shows that only 4% of their relationship happened by chance. Note that I used semi-annual values and did first-order differencing to minimize the impact of seasonality.

If we use the P/B Ratio, the price is not that cheap. It currently trades at 1.24x its BVPS, which is still quite higher than the five-year average. This also suggests a TP of $3.34, justifying the continued price downtrend and showing a $3.34 downside potential.

DCF Model

The DCF Model suggests that TRVG is not that cheap for its intrinsic value. It gives a TP of $3.53, a 2.7% upside potential.

Like most of my coverage on Seeking Alpha, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2% to be consistent with inflation. Meanwhile, I derived its WACC using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. As you can see, it is the same as the cost of equity because TRVG has no borrowings. I also set its risk premium at 5% as my minimum rate to compensate for the risk investors must take if they decide to buy this.

I derived its FCFF by projecting future values of NOPAT, depreciation, capex, and net working capital. I computed its revenue by getting its CAGR for the past five years, which is 9% and used it as the revenue growth rate. Then, I set COGS (2.9%) and operating expenses (95.7%) as a percent of revenue using their five-year average. For depreciation and capex, I computed it using the straight-line method. I didn’t assume the company to buy new equipment to make it simpler. Lastly, I estimated its net working capital by setting its operating assets and liabilities as a percent of revenue. I only used the operating assets and liabilities. Accounts receivables and payables were exceptions since I applied DSO and DPO to them.

Technicals

Technicals show TRVG as a risky option. Its price just fell below the 50-day SMA, showing that recent trends have become a bit pessimistic. Although both are above the 200-day SMA, investors must watch out for a potential bearish reversal.

This can be supported by the bearish divergence spotted last March 21 after setting a new 52-week high. However, buying volume weakened considerably, as shown by MACD and RSI. Their most recent high was lower than the previous one, suggesting uncertainties. True enough, the price took a nosedive, declining by 33% in just three weeks. And I think the trend may continue as it already breached the previous support of $3.97. In addition, it is still above the oversold region. This trend seems to give logic to my target price.

Conclusion

trivago N.V. shows a notable improvement in Q4 with its topline growth and increased liquidity. However, a lot of sustainability concerns must be addressed amid the shifting consumer preferences and tightening competition. In the long run, it may erode its value as more customers and travelers rely more on direct bookings. It is also not worth the risk considering the fact that it’s still not cheap and the evident technical risks. I am giving this a sell rating for not offering added value to your portfolio.

data.title: The World Is Selling America
data.description:
By Brien Lundin

The world is worried. And typically in such an environment, investors across the globe rush to three safe havens: the U.S. dollar, U.S. Treasuries, and gold.

This time is different - because they’re dumping the greenback and Treasuries... and buying gold hand over fist.

This dynamic isn’t just unusual, it’s unprecedented...and illustrated in the chart below.

In this chart, the Dollar Index is in green, the 10-year Treasury yield is in red, and gold, of course, is the gold area in the background. The bottom panel is the 20-day rolling correlation between the dollar and Treasury yields.

In normal times, the dollar and yields are positively correlated, with the correlation line above zero. That brief dip in the correlation in late March merely reflects some back-and-forth, trendless action earlier in the month.

Now note the sharp divergence over the last week when, counter to the Trump administration’s hopes and plans, their harsh tariff policies sent the world running from the U.S.....and toward the one remaining safe haven of gold.

The dollar sank, Treasury yields jumped and the gold price soared.

Put simply, the rest of the world is selling America.

Importantly, as the chart also shows clearly, that flight from U.S. safe havens into gold ended the metal’s brief correction and sent the price skyward, wiping out much of its overbought status in the process.

On Monday, gold was slammed on the U.S. open, as investors flocked back to U.S. equities because President Trump delayed his reciprocal tariffs and exempted electronics exported from China from his most onerous levies.

The steady-state demand from central banks along with investors and institutions around the world is still in place, though, and sparking a rebound from the early-session price lows.

Mining Stocks Take Off

A soaring gold price is nothing new — we’ve seen this kind of action over and over since February of last year.

What’s different this time is that Western investors are now coming to the party...finding the move in gold far advanced...and deciding to play the trend via gold mining stocks.

The result is that, even with gold rocketing higher, the gold stocks have been doing even better. This chart of the GDX gold mining index/gold price ratio shows the dramatic outperformance of the miners.

Even more impressive, the gold stock indices are up again, even with gold down about 1%.

It’s an amazing time - fraught with risks and tremendous opportunities.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: April's 5 Dividend Growth Stocks With Yields Up To 11.99%
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

For some background on this monthly publication, here is my view on dividend growth stocks:

Dividend growth stocks aren't always the most exciting investments out there. They often aren't grabbing the headlines, and they aren't the stocks running up hundreds of percentages in a year. In fact, they are often some of the least exciting stocks. And that is precisely their strongest selling point. With such a vast world of dividend growth stocks available out there, it is important to screen through to see if there are any worthwhile investments to explore.

They are stocks that provide growing wealth over time to income investors. Dividend growers are often larger (not always), more financially stable companies that can pay out reliable cash flows to investors. Some are slower growers than others. Some are going to be cyclical that require a strong economy. Some are going to be secular, which doesn't generally rely on a more robust economy.

Dividend growth can promote share price appreciation. Of course, that is if these companies are growing their earnings to support such dividend growth in the first place. Trust me. There are yield traps out there - I've owned a few that I'm not particularly proud of.

I like to think of investing in dividend stocks as a perpetual loan of sorts. Essentially, every dividend is a repayment of your original capital. Eventually, holding long enough, you have the position "paid off." It is all returned back into your pocket from that point forward.

All of this being said, it is important to understand my approach to dividend stocks and why screening dividend stocks can be important for income investors. As with any initial screening, this is just an initial dive - more due diligence would be necessary before pulling the trigger.

The Parameters For Screening

I'll be using some handy features that Seeking Alpha provides right here on their website for this screen. In particular, I will be screening utilizing their quant grades in dividend safety, dividend growth and dividend consistency.

Dividend Safety is relatively self-explanatory. These will be stocks that SA quants show reasonable safety compared to the rest of their various sectors. The grade considers many different factors, but earnings payout ratios, debt, and free cash flow are among these. This category will be stocks with A+ to B- ratings.

For the dividend growth category, we have factors such as the CAGR of various periods relative to other stocks in the same sector. Additionally, the quants also look at earnings, revenue, and EBITDA growth. As we will see, this doesn't mean that every stock with a higher grade has the growth we are looking for. This just factors in that the dividend has grown or earnings are growing to support possible dividend growth. For these, the grades will also be A+ through B- ratings.

Finally, for dividend consistency, we want stocks that will be paying reliable dividends for us for a very long time. In particular, hopefully, they are raising yearly, though that isn't an explicit requirement. We will also include stocks with a general uptrend in dividend payments, which means there could have been periods where they paused increases for a year or two.

After looking at those factors alone, we are left with 456 stocks at this time from the 431 listed last month. I'll link the screen here, though it is a dynamic list that constantly updates regularly. When viewing this article, there could be more or less when going to the link.

From there, I wanted to narrow down the list a lot more. I then sorted the list by forward dividend yield, from highest to lowest. Since these will be safer dividend stocks in the first place, screening for those among the higher payers shouldn't hurt. Though there are always exceptions and I try to note those.

Data was pulled as of April 3, 2025—since then, the market has been incredibly volatile, so checking the latest market data before making any investment decision is even more important than usual. Additional due diligence is always required, as this is just an initial screening.

As usual, we have some names that show up quite regularly, and we've provided quick updates in a fairly short period of time. In this case, it is just LyondellBasell Industries N.V. (LYB), which we covered last month.

Further, there are those names that come up on this screen that doesn't necessarily meet the spirit of this piece based on variable dividends or inconsistency when it comes to raising their dividend.

Artisan Partners Asset Management Inc. (APAM) is a name with a variable dividend, for example—while The Western Union Company (WU) is a name that just barely meets the screening for consistency, they actually haven't been raised for years. I also believe that with no growth on the horizon for their business, that isn't likely to change.

That leaves us with NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (OTCQX:NLCP), RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ), Hess Midstream LP (HESM), Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST) and United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS). These are all names we've touched on before, but it has been at least a quarter.

These are some riskier names currently due to pressures within their industries, that is primarily NLCP and UPS. RLJ and HST are also very sensitive to the overall economy. Given the latest tariff announcements, I'm certainly going to be more somber overall with the current economic outlook. HESM looks like the most solid of the names we'll be looking at today. I wanted to preface this before diving in, so with that, let's look at these speculative names and why they are speculative.

NewLake Capital Partners, Inc. (NCLP) 11.99% Yield

NLCP trades OTC, so already that is a knock against this name. However, getting listed was something they had been working on for quite a while. As of the latest earnings call in early March, though, it appears that the requirements and the costs associated with complying were too great.

Regarding listing, after a careful analysis, we've determined that at this time, the structural requirements and associated costs of pursuing a TSX listing do not justify the benefits. We will, of course, revisit this evaluation if circumstances change, and we know liquidity is critically important, and we will continue to seek options and work hard to enhance liquidity and trading of our stock.

So, it would seem that the idea is on the sidelines for now. Being listed on the NYSE or Nasdaq would provide significant liquidity as there are restrictions on institutional investors in terms of what they can generally invest in.

Further, while NLCP might not be in the worst spot, it's much larger and NYSE-listed competitor, Innovative Industrial Properties, Inc. (IIPR), is having a very tough time with its tenants. This highlights just how risky and speculative this area is. Initially, IIPR's largest tenant was in default and just as those issues were starting to get resolved, more tenants were recently announced as being in default.

I'm long IIPR, but I fully expect a dividend cut at this point, as there was a path previously that could potentially be avoided, but this is probably too much for them to navigate now. This obviously doesn't bode well for NLCP, but on the slightly positive side, NLCP carries nearly no leverage, and similarly, IIPR is lowly leveraged as well.

For now, that ~12% yield remains in place as they declared the same dividend for the first quarter, and it remains quite attractive. Though with tenant issues popping up in its competitor, we are clearly on shaky ground.

RLJ Lodging Trust (RLJ) 7.54% Yield

RLJ is a name we first had on our radar in last November's monthly screening piece. This is a real estate investment trust that is focused on hotel properties, more specifically, on premium branded hotel rooms with "high-margin, focused-service and compact full-service hotels." They are also quite small noting only 96 hotels in 23 states (and DC). They also have an "ownership interest in one unconsolidated hotel with 171 rooms."

As would be expected, this makes them economically sensitive. In particular, during the Covid pandemic, they were hit hard, and that caused them to slash their dividend for a couple of years down to the token penny. In more recent years, they've begun to ramp it back up quite significantly as well.

However, with trade wars on the potential horizon, after the recent tariffs being announced, we could be due for a slowdown. Being that RLJ operates exclusively in the U.S., it might be relatively sheltered in terms of direct impact—it would be more of the indirect impact of the overall economic slowdown that is likely being caused that would be more of a worry.

Hess Midstream LP (HESM) 6.54% Yield

After touching on two more speculative names and two more to go after this, HESM here looks like a solid potential watchlist name. This is a midstream company, and while it is an "LP," there is no K-1. The last K-1 came to investors in 2019, and since they've converted to an up-C structure.

It has come up quite regularly; the last time we touched on this name was last October. Since then, shares have been moving higher, pushing that attractive yield down to ~6.5% from the ~7.20% level that we were at previously.

That is even despite their continued growth of the payout during this period. One appealing part of HESM for dividend growth investors is that they raise their distribution every quarter.

However, like anything in investing, it isn't without its own risks. Midstreams operate in a toll-like way due to mostly fixed-rate contracts. With HESM, though, they have a concentration in that their largest customer by far is Hess Corporation (HES), which accounts for almost 90% of its earnings. Further muddying the waters is the Chevron Corporation (CVX) takeover of HES, which adds more moving parts to consider.

HESM has indicated in a past earnings call that it isn't expected to disrupt their business too much, as the contract structure should remain in place.

In the fourth quarter of 2023, Hess announced that it entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by Chevron Corporation.

Hess Midstream expects upon consummation of the proposed transaction, Chevron will acquire Hess's 37.8% ownership in Hess Midstream, including its right to appoint four directors to the board of Hess Midstream. Hess midstream's contract structure remains in place.

Host Hotels & Resorts, Inc. (HST) 6.14% Yield

Back to the relatively more sensitive names in terms of pressure from broader economics, we are looking at another hotel-focused REIT. In this case, they note that they are the largest lodging REIT in the world. They are investment-grade rated and note that they own 81 hotels. Of course, that is an even lower number than RLJ, and we noted that they were a small REIT. However, over the 81 hotels, HST has 43,400 rooms—RLJ is less than half of that with 21,200.

Another way to look at this is their enterprise value, with HST being roughly 4.4x larger than RLJ.

Still, even given its size difference, it wasn't enough to be able to withstand the Covid pandemic. This REIT is also quite a bit older, going back to the mid-90s. That gives us plenty of a track record to tell us that HST couldn't handle Covid, but it also couldn't handle the Global Financial Crisis, nor the fallout from the Dot-com bubble—at least in terms of being able to maintain a steady growing dividend. Instead, they slashed it down to nothing during this period.

They also seem to grow to this $0.20 quarterly level, then transition to specials instead of growing it any further. That's normally a solid strategy for a business that is so volatile and uncertain. That can give the management team and the Board some more room to cut back on their specials while maintaining the regulars.

However, as we can see, that strategy hasn't been enough for HST as they actually weren't even able to support the regular during these times. So it's a fairly good theory, but it isn't working out in practice, at least not in this field—the business development company space is an example of an area where it actually tends to work out as intended.

Given that and the expectation for lower AFFO in the coming year before rebounding some, there is some concern here. We aren't probably looking at needing to cut the regular yet, but that will depend on what eventually happens with these tariffs.

United Parcel Service, Inc. (UPS) 5.95% Yield

UPS was a name that wasn't a regular on this monthly screening piece, but over the last six months or so has been very regular due to the share price declines the stock has been experiencing.

UPS is another name that is particularly sensitive to economic conditions and consumers being strong. With fewer packages moving in a slower economy, that's likely to hurt their business. They've already been seeing some significant pressure on their earnings after the pandemic surge.

However, despite the pressures that UPS is facing, they still meet the criteria to be included in today's discussion. They also appear to want to keep that 15-year dividend streak of growth alive as they bumped it up by a penny this year.

Of course, we can also see that they massively raised the dividend shortly after the pandemic, and that might have been getting a bit too ahead of themselves at the time. As we saw right after that, earnings began to decline sharply. Analysts believe that 2024 was the low point, and we could begin to see a turnaround from here.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: For The U.S., Trump's 'Beautiful' Tariffs Are In The Eye Of The Beholder
data.description:
By James Knightley

Trump's tariffs are a tool to achieve three objectives

President Donald Trump sees “beautiful” tariffs as a tool to achieve three objectives:

  1. A diplomatic weapon to change the behaviour of foreign nations.
  2. An incentive for manufacturing reshoring to the US.
  3. A way of raising revenue to fund future tax cuts for households and corporates.

The announcement of a 10% minimum tariff that jumps to 25% for some products and then rises steeply to 145% for the majority of Chinese exports may, over time, reap benefits for the US economy and American workers. In the near term, though, it means the US economy faces a very challenging and likely economically damaging transition period.

If trade deals are done, then there is less scope for broader tax cuts

Financial markets have delivered their verdict, and the steep falls in equity and bond prices and heightened volatility suggest deep unease. Although tariffs have enhanced security at the Mexican and Canadian borders, they have also sparked resentment, leading to retaliatory tariffs and some instances of consumer boycotts against American products and services. For example, there has been a noticeable drop in Canadian flight bookings to the US.

At the same time, labour costs and the costs of constructing a new US plant, including transport and energy logistics, means that outside of high value-added, highly automated sectors, it is questionable how much reshoring will be delivered within the next couple of years. It may be cheaper in many instances to stick with foreign production centres and either absorb the costs within profit margins or pass higher costs on to customers.

With regards to the revenue-raising ability of tariffs, it appears the Administration is looking at around $600-700bn per year, which may cover the cost of the extension of Trump’s 2017 tax cuts that are set to sunset at the end of this year. However, taxpayers are not going to notice any change in their pay packets from this – it merely prevents a 2026 tax rise. Any additional tax cuts – not taxing tips and lower corporation tax (likely achievable) plus not taxing social security and overtime pay (less likely) – will not be felt until 2026 at the earliest. Of course, if deals are done and tariffs are scaled back, then there will be less fiscal headroom to deliver broader tax cuts.

Households are worried about spending power, stock market falls and the risk of losing their job

Growth headwinds intensify on multiple fronts

The concern over tariffs has led to a pre-emptive surge in auto, electronic and other big-ticket item sales in late March and early April, but the concern for upcoming months is that US consumers now face three major headwinds:

  • The erosion of spending power as goods prices jump (and by extension, some services, such as insurance and repair).
  • A rapidly cooling jobs market (ISM employment metrics contracting, Federal government lay-offs, quits rates pointing to wage growth slowing to 3%).
  • Falling equity markets and bond prices will involve negative wealth effects that predominantly deter higher-income households from spending.

So far, investment is holding up and should do enough to prevent a negative first-quarter GDP print, but the heightened economic uncertainty and falling equity prices mean businesses are likely to be reluctant to put money to work, so we expect that to cool noticeably. At the same time, government spending is set to slow markedly thanks to the cuts instigated by the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE).

Net trade will be a big drag on first-quarter GDP growth due to companies ramping up imports to get ahead of tariffs. In the second quarter, that will be less of a story, but reciprocal tariffs and consumer boycotts of US products remain a threat to export growth subsequently. Putting this together, we now look for full-year 2025 growth of 1.4% and 2026 growth of 1.1%, down from our previous forecasts of 2.1% and 1.6%, respectively.

Significant rate cuts from the summer

As for inflation, goods prices will inevitably increase, and we expect headline inflation to rise above 4%. We will also see some service prices rise too, such as insurance, but the shelter components, which account for 35% of the inflation basket by weight and more than 40% for core inflation, will come under downward pressure later in the year.

Rising unemployment and hurting household finances mean landlords won’t be able to keep pushing rents up 5%. The Cleveland Fed’s measure of national new rent agreements is already falling in year-on-year terms, and given the transmission mechanism, we think the Fed will become increasingly comfortable with the view that inflation will be back towards 2% by the end of 2026.

Fed Chair Jerome Powell has already indicated that he sees the threat of more persistent inflation and weaker growth ahead, but having delivered 100bp of rate cuts already, and with little sign of financial system stress, the Federal Reserve is prepared to “wait and see.” We think the Fed will start cutting meaningfully from the third quarter and expect 125bp of cuts.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Snowflake: Valuation Is Running Way Too Hot Amid Slowing Growth (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Recent market volatility has put a gaping dent in many momentum stocks, and perhaps no tech company has fallen faster and farther from grace than Snowflake (NYSE:SNOW), the cloud data warehousing company.

During the pandemic, Snowflake was one of the market’s hottest trades, regularly commanding valuation multiples above 20x revenue. Now, even before tariff related anxieties set in, Snowflake was already slumping in 2024 as other growth stocks rallied, as IT budget moderation impacted its usage rates and hurt its growth trends.

Tariff scares have dropped Snowflake further, with investors worrying that a softer global economy will encourage even more economizing on IT spending. We have to ask ourselves, however - with Snowflake down ~25% from YTD peaks above $190 (and still at a fraction of pandemic-era highs above $390), how much of the risk is already priced in? In my view: not enough.

I last wrote a neutral opinion on Snowflake in January, when the stock was trading closer to $160. Since then, Snowflake shares have dropped further: but so have its growth rates, as well as its expectations for growth in the upcoming year. While I believe in the strength of Snowflake’s business over the long haul, the company is heavily subject to oscillating demand trends in the near term. On top of this, the recent market correction has turned so many of Snowflake’s peers into true value stocks. Snowflake may be cheaper, but not on a relative basis against more-decimated peers in the software sector. I also expect that, with a highly variable consumption-based pricing model, Snowflake may revise downward its expectations for FY26 in conjunction with recent geopolitical turmoil. With all this in mind, I’m dropping my rating on the stock to a sell.

Since valuation is my biggest gripe against the stock, let’s start with where Snowflake is trading against the latest expectations. At current share prices near $145, Snowflake trades at a market cap of $48.23 billion. After we net off the $5.30 billion of cash and $2.27 billion of convertible debt on Snowflake’s latest balance sheet, the company’s resulting enterprise value is $45.20 billion.

Meanwhile, for FY26 (the year for Snowflake ending in January 2026), management has guided to $4.28 billion in product revenue, or 24% y/y growth - wherein product revenue typically represents 95-96% of the company’s total. Wall Street, meanwhile, is expecting all-in revenue of $4.48 billion in FY26, also 24% y/y growth. This positions Snowflake’s valuation at 10.0x EV/FY26 revenue.

Double-digit revenue multiples have become an exception, rather than the norm, in the software sector - unlike in the pandemic era days. Snowflake bulls can try to argue that the company’s mid-20s growth rate justifies a high multiple - but the question is if this pace can be sustained.

As a reminder, Snowflake is one of very few software companies that insists that it is not on a SaaS business model. It bills its customers based on how much data they actually consume, rather than most software products like Salesforce (CRM) or Workday (WDAY) that charge per month, per head (often what is called a seat-based pricing model).

The result is that Snowflake’s demand is actually quite elastic. Snowflake itself doesn’t face any direct tariff impacts - but its customer base certainly does. We already saw evidence of tighter IT budget controls dent Snowflake’s revenue growth and net retention rates in FY25. My concern is that stiffening macro headwinds will increase the pressure on Snowflake and cause the company to drop its guidance for the year. Again, as a consumption-based company Snowflake’s management has less visibility into the revenue pipeline than seat-based software products. This can make for bigger positive surprises during boom times, but the opposite during tough times. I think we’re in for the latter.

Steer clear here and sell your position in Snowflake to make room in your portfolio for more value-oriented software stocks.

Q4 download

Let’s now go through Snowflake’s latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q4 earnings summary is shown below:

Snowflake’s revenue grew 27% y/y in the quarter to $986.8 million, ahead of Wall Street’s expectations of $956.3 million (+23% y/y) by a four point margin. Despite the top line beat, there are a number of worrying signals that investors should observe cautiously.

The first is continued slippage in the company’s net revenue retention rate, which indicates at what level Snowflake’s customers are boosting their spending on the platform. A high NRR is a key feature of consumption-based software products, and while it’s true that Snowflake’s NRR remains high relative to other software peers, it’s in a declining trend. The chart below shows NRR in Q4 at 126%, one point weaker than 127% in Q3 and four points weaker than 131% in the year-ago Q4.

Again, expansion is based on customers deciding to consume more data and spend more, which typically happens when companies are investing in infrastructure projects or building new apps. Calendar Q1, meanwhile, has seen a slippage of consumer spending and business confidence. In my view, this foreshadows that the budget optimization that hurt Snowflake in FY25 may spill over and impact NRRs further in FY26.

Another concern: customer expansion may be slowing. The chart below shows that Snowflake added 543 customers sequentially in the fourth quarter to end with 11.2k customers, but within the larger G2000 segment, Snowflake added only 3 net new customers.

This exposes twin risks for Snowflake. Net expansion from existing customers is slowing, driven by macro headwinds and budget optimization - but the company’s new business engine also seems to be stalling. It’s likely that these trends will shift Snowflake’s growth trajectory meaningfully, which will impact its valuation multiples.

Watch out for a slowdown in Q1 too, and Snowflake’s apparent resistance to cost-cutting

The next major catalyst for Snowflake will be its Q1 earnings release, expected sometime in late April (no date set yet). For Q1, the company has guided to a further slowdown to 21-22% growth in product revenue, which represents up to six points of sequential deceleration versus Q3.

Amid the expected slowdown, we note that Snowflake has been resistant to cost cuts. In FY25, the company’s headcount swelled to 7.8k, representing a 12% y/y rise. This resulted in a drop in operating margins for the full year to 6%, two points worse than 8% in FY24:

Typically, we like to see companies turn their focus to profitability in a time of slowing growth. Salesforce is the most notable example of this: after years of chasing growth via acquisitions and huge hiring, Marc Benioff led the company to a 180-degree pivot and is now focusing on the bottom line, leading to meaningful EPS expansion.

Snowflake has discussed eliminating management layers and driving AI to automate more work, but we have yet to see any results at all in improving its margins. Sridhar Ramaswamy, now beginning his second year as Snowflake’s CEO, noted that the company is planning for further hiring in engineering and sales in FY26 on the Q4 earnings call:

You'll also see our emphasis on rigor and efficiency in our growth plan. Our headcount growth over the next year is focused on engineering and sales, which are teams with direct impact to revenue. We are using AI internally to drive efficiency in incredible ways. Our teams are able to tap into a wealth of data and information faster than ever before. It is this operational rigor that we instituted in fiscal '25 that is now our way of life going forward, investing in growth while having a maniacal focus on driving efficiencies throughout our business."

In the coming Q1 earnings release, we should watch for any incremental news from the company on efficiency plans (or more likely, the lack thereof). Currently, guidance is only calling for 8% pro forma operating margins for FY26, which would essentially only revert Snowflake back to FY24 profit margin levels.

Key takeaways

With expensive valuation multiples, slowing growth and net revenue retention rates subject to a pullback in customer spending, and rapid hiring that is preventing margin progression, Snowflake is looking very shaky in 2025 amid any tariff-related macro fallouts.

Watch out here: in my view, we haven’t seen the bottom yet in this stock.

data.title: Shinhan Financial: Focus On Q1 Earnings And Peer Valuation Gap
data.description:
I still have a bullish opinion of Shinhan Financial Group Co., Ltd.(NYSE:SHG) (055550.KS) or "SFGCL".

My prior January 21, 2025, update touched on the company's financial outlook and its stance on cash returns.

The current write-up previews SFGCL's upcoming earnings and considers its re-rating potential.

The bank's Q1FY25 results should exceed expectations based on my analysis. I also think that the stock can command a similar valuation as KB Financial Group Inc. (KB) (105560.KS) or "KFGI".

I'm Expecting Solid Bottom-Line Expansion

Shinhan will report its 1Q25 financials on April 24 (Source: S&P Capital IQ). I think that the bank will do well in absolute terms and also offer a pleasant surprise for investors.

My projections point to SHG's normalized net profit rising by +10% YoY to ₩1,454B in the initial three months of this year.

The company had suffered from earnings contractions of -5% and -14% for 1Q24 and 4Q24, respectively on YoY terms. The market will be pleased to see it return to positive growth territory. My net income growth forecast is also 2 ppts ("percentage points") better than the consensus estimate of +8% according to S&P Capital IQ data.

The bank's first-quarter performance should have been boosted by 3 factors.

One of them is margin improvement.

I see SFGCL's NIM ("Net Interest Margin") rising by +4 bps ("basis points") between 4Q2024 and 1Q2025 due to reduced Cost of Funds ("CoF").

Listed South Korean ("SK") financial institutions in general have intentionally held back on RWA ("Risk-Weighted Asset") growth. They want to prioritize the achievement of superior capital adequacy which can then support dividends and repurchases. This in turn restricts the competition for deposits in the industry.

SHG's goal is to have its CET-@ ("Common Equity Tier-1") metric stay at the 13% or higher level, which is what it registered in FY24. It has a similar motivation as other banks to be less aggressive on RWA expansion. That's positive for its CoF outlook.

Another driver is Credit Cost ("CC") normalization.

I assume that the bank has already made pre-emptive year-end provisioning. This implies that the CC burden should have eased by Q1. SFGCL's credit loss provisions jumped by +50% sequentially to KRW 603B in the last quarter of the previous year.

The final growth lever is non-interest income.

In a rate-cutting environment, there is the potential for SHG to realize gains associated with the company's bonds. This is because the valuation of fixed-income assets increases when interest rates go lower.

My take is that an earnings beat can act as a short-term catalyst for Shinhan.

SFGCL Could Trade On Par With KB

There is a disparity in valuations between KFGI And SHG. The former is trading at 0.63x P/NAV. The market has chosen to assign a wider 55% discount to book value for the latter ("0.45x P/B").

The gap between the two was much less significant in the past.

In the last decade, SHG's mean P/B premium over KB Financial was 7%. In the 2022-2024 time frame, Shinhan traded -6% ("on average") lower than KFGI based on the same ratio.

There are various metrics supporting a more demanding valuation for SHG that's in line with its peer.

Both of them have made decent progress in diversifying their business mix. Non-banking assets represented a quarter of their respective balance sheets as of December 31, 2024.

The actual FY24 Returns on Equity ("ROEs") for the two firms were the same at 9%. A stock does warrant a bigger premium to book, if it can earn a higher return on its net assets. This isn't the case for KB.

Also, income investors might even prefer SFGCL over KFGI. Shinhan's consensus next twelve months' dividend yield of 5.0% is 50bps higher than that for KB. If more yield-hunters buy SHG's shares, this could push up its stock price in the future.

The long-term financial goals for these banks are aligned as well. They are targeting a capital return ratio of at least half, a minimum ROE in the teens, and a CET1 of more than 13 percent.

Risks I Would Watch

There are worst-case scenarios for Shinhan that one needs to consider. A Q1 earnings miss might drive the company's share price down in late April. Also, the valuations of Korean equities and the domestic banking sector might de-rate, in the event of a global recession or bear market.

My Closing Thoughts

I have identified two major catalysts that make SHG a buy.

Shinhan will likely report positive bottom-line growth again in the latest quarter. This should serve as a near-term re-rating trigger.

There's also a good chance that investors will be willing to value the company's shares at a higher multiple. SFGCL's 29% ("0.45/0.63") P/B discount vis-a-vis its peers is unwarranted, when there are more similarities than differences between the two.

data.title: Palantir Is Hiding In Plain Sight: Maintaining The Stock With A Buy
data.description:
I last wrote about Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) in late February when the stock was trading at $106 per share. The stock is down 12.8% since my article, against the S&P 500 (SP500), down around 11.6% during the same period. My thesis still stands, and I hereon reiterate Palantir with a buy and think every pullback is a gift that keeps on giving. Palantir is a stock I’ve been optimistic about since late September last year when the stock was trading around $36 per share, and it is up around 150% since then, as seen below.

I like the company for the long term, and I continue to advise investors to add on each and every pullback for a lower cost average. I think there’s more upside ahead in the near term after NATO’s operational headquarters committed to using Palantir’s Maven Smart System for military planning within the next 30 days. The acquisition was finalized on March 25, and the contract value wasn’t disclosed. According to NATO Communications and Information Agency General Manager Ludwig Decamps, the new tech will provide

“customized state-of-the-art AI capabilities to the Alliance, and empowering our forces with the tools required on the modern battlefield to operate effectively and decisively.”

I’ve been tracking the Maven Smart System since early November last year after the company signed a five-year contract to expand the system’s AI/ML capabilities across the U.S. military service, including the Army, Air Force, Navy, and U.S. Marine Corps. Quick reminder: customers can give a workload that “required 2,000 staff back during Operation Iraqi Freedom to only require 20 people.” The stock closed up around 5% higher on Monday after the news; Maven started as a classified DoD project but is now becoming the operating system of the West. Palantir has been hiding in plain sight.

Valuation and price movement:

The stock has a beautiful setup, in my opinion, and everything is pointing towards a reversal.

Palantir stock is currently trading at $92.6, above its EMA21 at $86.7, EMA50 at $86.4, and EMA200 at $65. If you look at the chart above, you probably see a few bullish signs right off the bat, with RSI currently in the neutral zone at 50 and the MACD bullish cross, along with these green circles you see above, that I consider a cool bonus. While my thesis is more long-term, I think it’s also important to get in at the lowest price, hence why I also like to predict the price movement based on signs. Fortunately for Palantir bulls, this looks like a good time to add on the pullback as I expect the stock to shoot up from here. These are the levels I’m watching like a hawk:

  1. $86-$88 for a prime add zone where the EMAs are currently forming a cluster.

  2. $91-$92 near-term support level, and $66-$67 for a long-term support level.

  3. $97 is the breakout level for the near term.

  4. $105-$110 as a long-term target zone.

  5. $125 is the all-time high retest zone.

Volume is also spiking up, another bullish sign.

The stock is extremely expensive, according to its valuation metrics, even after the pullback. The stock is trading at 225.9x P/E and 166.9 forward P/E, against the sector median of 19.6x and 18.8x, respectively. EV/Sales ratio is also inflated, with forward EV/Sales at 56.2x, against the sector median at 2.4x, and price/sales and price/book tell the same story.

And if we were to put these metrics aside for a second, the company’s numbers in growth and profitability are a sight for sore eyes. The company’s year-over-year revenue growth of 29% is ahead of the sector median (to say the least) at 5%, with EPS growth at 41%. On profitability, the company’s gross profit margin of 80% exceeds the sector median at 50%, and its net income margin of 16% is also ahead of the sector at 4%.

Investment thesis and what’s next:

I’d argue you get what you pay for: an AI software company that, through its AIP, has no competition yet, making it a first mover in the space, with no alternative product in the market offering Palantir’s AI-governed operational command.

So while the company has competitors in AI and data analytics from Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) and Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Palantir’s AIP integrates data for action-driven decisions uniquely, making it perfect for high-stakes sectors like healthcare and defense. No one has come close, and that’s why you see the company’s edge in government contracts.

My old thesis was three parted, and I’m glad to say last quarter showed momentum picking up. I expect acceleration in both commercial and government revenue to continue into the next quarter. While the company started with government revenue making the big chunk of its business, it slowly but surely diversified into commercial revenue that currently accounts for 45% of its total revenue, up 64% year over year in 4Q24. The company continued its strategy and announced two deals in early March, the first with TWG Global and the second with Société Générale. I expect these to show up in the commercial side of the business over the next 2 quarters.

Additionally, while I was expecting increased new customer conversions and expansions in existing customers through Warp Speed as a long-term tailwind, it’s coming sooner than expected as the company announced six new customers deploying Warp Speed, including Epirus, Red Cat, Saildrone, Saronic, Usra Major, and SNC.

I think Maven is proving closer than ever too, certainly more with the latest NATO news. This doesn’t only concern Palantir, but reflects the border economic and political uncertainty as Europe looks to lessen its dependence on the U.S. for defense.

Palantir’s 1Q25 earnings are coming in early May, on the 5th, to be specific. I expect the company to beat on top and bottom lines and beat higher for 2Q25 as I don’t see headwinds from tariffs and the DeepSeek, from Hangzhou DeepSeek Artificial Intelligence Co., Ltd. (DEEPSEEK) moment on the company. I discussed DeepSeek further in my previous article, so I’m saving you the refresher there.

I’m not optimistic on a lot of stocks like I am on Palantir, and for good reason. Though Palantir has flown under the radar for a while, I think the market is finally awakening to the beast in its midst.

data.title: Nvidia: Already On The Way Back Up After Tariffs Turmoil
data.description:
Just when I thought in my previous article on Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) in which I highlighted that the going has been tough, I certainly didn't expect the market to take me for another wild ride into the abyss, as both the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq threatened to be engulfed in a gut-wrenching bear market.

While I telegraphed the long-term upside for the stock, NVDA isn’t immune to short-term swings as the market rotated out of growth related plays recently. Fortuitously, we have been spared from a further drubbing (at least for now), as President Trump provided much-needed respite through his now famous/infamous “Blink Wednesday,” as he communicated a 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs targeting America’s trading partners (aside from China).

In addition, Trump has also extended relief to a broad range of electronic products that would have caused massive pain among US consumers, especially since China is a major exporting country to the US, as the big tech and their partners have developed well-established supply chain in China, that’s arguably nearly impossible to replicate or reshore in the short term. Hence, the back and forth is in full display again for investors, although I’m not about to complain since a further reckoning in the market could have set off greater panic in the market, especially as cracks in the financial system appeared to be forming.

For Nvidia, the respite is very much welcomed, even though a permanent relief remains unlikely. The White House has already triggered an investigation into semiconductors imports, which will likely see the industry being carved out for its own set of tariffs, similar to what the automakers have already been dealing with.

Yet, the silver lining is that even Trump appears to be prepared to remain “flexible” in auto tariffs, as the automakers could see their profits being decimated as the impact in the supply chain starts to percolate. Hence, the threat of them passing on higher costs to consumers is almost a given (the questions now moves on to how much?), which could set off alarm bells about whether higher inflationary expectations could be anchored, complicating the Fed’s responses into lowering interest rates for the rest of the year in view of possibly slowing economic growth.

In other words, I believe the market is likely more assured that while the imposition of semi tariffs is a clear and present danger to the semiconductor supply chain, the president seems prepared for temporary reprieve if the key players could commit to reshoring their manufacturing bases into the US. The White House might deem it imperative to take control of semi manufacturing given its importance to national security, particularly as the US-China trade war is expected to worsen, as the tit-for-tat measures don’t appear to have a clear off-ramp for either party at the moment.

Trump’s punitive tariffs rates on China has compelled Xi Jinping to dig in, while appealing to key trading partners in Southeast Asia to rally around China’s plight, and aiming to extend bilateral cooperation to prevent the US from forming a defensive perimeter through economic coercion, undermining China’s economic interests.

For Nvidia, while its reliance on China revenue has arguably slowed down, CEO Jensen Huang’s appeal to President Trump personally through the Mar-a-Lago diplomacy has yielded positive results, as Trump seems prepared to allow Nvidia to continue selling its H20 AI chips to China’s leading tech companies, in exchange for the Nvidia to commit to a $500B investment in America for domestic manufacturing.

Could these already feature planned expansion opportunities strategically interwoven to fit into Trump’s narrative, like how Apple’s own $500B investment plan appears to be so. Since CapEx discussion and execution takes far more than a few months, it doesn’t seem likely that Nvidia’s announcement is in response to Trump’s reciprocal tariffs, and surely not to the tune of $500B for two weeks’ work. Hence, I believe Jensen has delivered a masterclass rivaling Apple (AAPL) CEO Tim Cook, which even President Trump alluded to in his remarks as he named Cook in comments regarding the recent reprieve provided to electronic products that surely benefited Apple.

However, with the compliance deadline for the AI diffusion rule set to live in May 2025, it could portend another period of apprehension for Nvidia, given its immense exposure to AI data center revenue (almost 90% of its last twelve months total revenue base). The industry has already called out that the wording of the framework in its current form is detrimental to help maintain US long-term leadership in AI, given the marked impact that it could cause onto key allies and partners outside of the US.

I’ve also enunciated just how Alibaba (BABA) appears to be primed to capitalize on any blowback from the rules implementation that could result in less AI computing capacity, hindering the growth cadence of the US hyperscale behemoths. Hence, it isn't without basis that China’s tech giants are prepared to take advantage of a pullback imposed by the rules, possibly hurting Nvidia and the hyperscalers.

It’s already a known fact that China's AI capabilities are making leaps and bounds in their progress since DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) threw a spanner in the works for America’s leading AI firms earlier this year. So much so that even OpenAI has decided that it’s timely for the company to release open-source models to keep up with the narrative and broaden the adoption in enterprise companies looking to lower their barriers to entry as they experiment and implement AI in their workflow.

Still, the technological marvel of Nvidia’s Blackwell architecture ramp has been progressing well, and TSMC’s (TSM) recent monthly revenue results validates that the structural gains in AI aren’t expected to slow down anytime soon. Deepwater Asset Management also argued that we are still a few years away from a possible AI bubble burst, as Agentic AI takes centerstage in 2025, although we haven’t yet quite see that thesis taking off yet.

Perhaps, business leaders are increasingly worried about the policy flip-flops and abrupt changes in the White House’s policy formulations and implementations that there could be a pause in spending, which might temper the AI enthusiasm (but not cancellation). Yet, given Nvidia’s sensitivity to data center AI spending, it remains to be seen whether Microsoft’s (MSFT) decision to adjust its CapEx footprint might be construed as a broader downturn, although Google (GOOGL) (GOOG) appears to have struck back at such worries with CEO Sundar Pichai committing to its planned CapEx for 2025.

Yet, the question about 2026 could come to the fore in Nvidia’s subsequent management outlook, especially companies are expected to provide less robust or even withhold guidance in view of the economic restructuring that is going on right now. Such ambiguities could morph into decision paralysis if we aren’t careful, and Nvidia’s outlook for 2026 would likely be impacted, although Nvidia's earnings estimates are still well-poised for growth.

In addition, we must also reflect doubts into the execution of semiconductor tariffs, as the extent and implementation timeline remains unknown for now. However, as I mentioned earlier, the opportunity to obtain short-term or even rolling reprieves might be possible, although Trump may demand further spending commitments in exchange for such a breather. How these could affect Nvidia’s outlay and whether its supply chain partners can manage these changes while maintaining profitability remains a big question mark for now.

Despite that, it would be unusual if the market hasn’t reacted to these uncertainties, particularly since they may present substantial obstacles on Nvidia’s AI-driven proposition. And the market is clearly not foolish, as NVDA’s forward EBITDA multiple has dropped to 20.5x, well below its 10Y average of 33.7x. With a forward PEG ratio of 0.66 (more than 50% below its sector median), has the market turned too gloomy on its growth outlook and overplayed Nvidia’s fundamentally strong thesis, as it fends off competitive risks from hyperscalers custom design, while taking on Broadcom (AVGO) as the semiconductor bellwether attempts to disrupt Nvidia’s market dominance.

And we also cannot rule out AMD (AMD) as it seeks to gain its place in the AI value chain, while Intel’s (INTC) takes another stab at its resurgence under CEO Lip-Bu Tan’s leadership. However, I believe the market is justified to feel aggrieved with the geopolitical convolutions engendered by Trump’s widening trade war which could return to haunt us as we get closer to the 90-day deadline on reciprocal tariffs, with little deal resolution with China in the works, while still navigating treacherous terrain on semiconductor sectoral tariffs at the same time.

While buying sentiments on NVDA appears to remain robust last week as the stock seems to have bottomed out, the trend bias could move into a downtrending phase if we cannot hold the $90 decisively, even though NVDA’s valuation is arguably reasonably attractive when adjusted for its potential growth.

Hence, I believe the $75 level might even be in play if the recovery in the market hits a stumbling block, as the market possibly adjusts to a much slower pace of growth for AI enterprise spending, as customers might also reach out to lower priced chips offered by competitors or hyperscalers, although that isn’t my base case for now.

Nvidia’s ability to maintain its growth prospects through incessant innovation as showcased by its recent GTC demonstrates how it has managed to carve out its leadership over its archrivals. Yet, the company’s efforts to amplify the adoption of AI might face a stuttering block as the US government’s initiatives to reorder trade flows and manufacturing exposure could have global ramifications that might send the US economy into a recession, possibly torpedoing NVDA’s valuation multiples.

While these risks are highlighted to remind you to consider them as we take into account its growth prospects, I believe the risk/reward remains primed to the upside, supported by its market dominance, the White House’s support for global AI leadership, NVDA’s battered growth adjusted valuation, and also a possible bottom from last week’s lows.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XLU: Safe Haven Assets Could Prove Even More Attractive (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLU) delivered a strong rally since my previous coverage. The fund has been standing on the green territory since the beginning of the year, while the broad market and the technology sector are in a prominent downfall. Last time I based my Hold rating on the estimated lower upside potential of the utility stocks, which were overshadowed by the technology players. However, the new tariff conditions changed the market situation in favor of the utility sector, which could prove to be an attractive safe haven for investors amid the uncertain macro environment. I believe that XLU has favorable potential in light of the expected energy consumption and capital investment pattern. My estimates give a clue that the fund could deliver up to a 20% total return for the next 12-month period, which makes me bullish on XLU again.

Investment Discussion

XLU offers an exposure to the Utilities Select Sector Index, which includes American utility companies engaged in the supply of electricity, gas, water, and steam.

The fund's industry allocation is focused mainly on electric utilities (64.6% share) and mixed utilities (26.9% share). However, the share of these two core pillars decreased slightly since my previous article in favor of independent power and renewable producers. It is not surprising, providing the growth of worldwide investments in clean energy sources is now almost twice as much as in fossil fuels. In the meantime, the ETF remained quite concentrated around its top 5 positions, which account for 38.4% of the portfolio and are distributed as follows: NextEra Energy (NEE) with 11.9% (vs. 13.7%); Southern Company (SO) with 8.2% (vs. 7.9%); Duke Energy (DUK) with 7.7% (unchanged); Constellation Energy (CEG) with 5.8% (vs. 6.4%); and American Electric Power (AEP) with a 4.7% share (vs. 4.5%).

As of April 9, 2025, the fund manages assets in the amount of $17 billion and charges the investors 0.08% on annum for the management. The AUM value grew by circa $5.4 billion from the date of my last coverage (approximately one year ago) due to the solid performance and a net inflow from investors in the amount of $2.7 billion.

Talking about the performance, last time I wrote that utilities continue to be outshined by the technology sector, which surged considerably thanks to the strong performance of the main chip players. However, from the following chart, we can notice that the state of affairs has changed.

XLU delivered a strong rally of circa 19% for the last 12-month period on the backdrop of a 6% gain in the S&P 500, represented by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), and a 3% drop in the broad Technology Select Sector SPDR (XLK). Two main factors for this could be the resilient earning growth of the utilities sector during 2024 and the fresh tariff policies from the new administration. The latter have a great impact on the technology sector due to the US reliance on imports of advanced electronic hardware from the Asian region. Meanwhile, utilities have limited exposure to the tariffs impact on the US economy, which is anticipated to experience a GDP slowdown and an uptick in consumer price inflation. In such an environment, the utilities sector could prove to be well-insulated, as the regulators usually permit passing along higher costs to customers.

Industry Potential

The growth prospects for the industry remain solid, as data centers are a key driver of energy consumption growth in the U.S. (see the chart below) in the coming years. The share of total energy consumption of data centers could reach up to 12% by 2028, which translates to a total power demand of up to 580 TWh. Moreover, the global IT spending in 2025 on data center systems is anticipated to surge by 23.2% YoY, being the fastest-growing segment of the overall IT.

In addition, the industrial sector could also contribute to the energy consumption growth. The U.S. is substantially reliant on advanced chip hardware imports, which, in light of the intensification of the tariff disputes, could only facilitate the commissioning of new semiconductor factories. Thus, the course towards developing domestic production will likely strengthen during the Trump presidency. TSMC already plans to increase U.S. investment in advanced semiconductor manufacturing by $100 billion with three new fabs.

At the same time, rising energy consumption stimulates capital investment expansion by the U.S. utility operators. The CAPEX is expected to accelerate prominently in the next few years, with $212 billion predicted for 2025, which represents a 22% increase from the $174 million spent in 2024. The growth of investments boosts the utilities' rate base, which creates conditions for higher revenue requirements and profit growth.

However, the uncertainty regarding the trade policy creates fears of an economic slowdown in the U.S. The current market sentiment implies four interest rate cuts of 25 bps over the next 12 months. A cut in the key interest rates will have a favorable impact on utility stocks by reducing the interest cost pressure. Moreover, it should lower the government bond yields and make these safe haven assets even more attractive to investors.

Examining the dividend yield of utilities and the monthly yield on 10Y treasuries, the current discrepancy between them seems to be the largest for the last three years. Concerning the dividend yield on XLU, which is 3% on a TTM basis, it could be explained by the solid performance of the utility stocks. On the other hand, the 10Y yield has climbed prominently since the first interest rate cut in September 2024. The main factor for this pattern could be strong economic growth and the slight uptick in inflation during the last year's final quarter. However, with the increased probability of a recession due to the new tariff provisions, the gap between the US10Y and the XLU yield could narrow.

Risk Factors

Natural disasters remain a significant concern, as they can cause damage to the utility infrastructure. The year started with massive fires in California, which led to a rapid drop in Edison International (EIX) shares. An investigation is currently underway into the possible involvement of faulty equipment of the company as a cause of the wildfire. In addition, the regulatory environment is becoming increasingly important for the utility business with the growth of investments in the energy infrastructure. The ability to pass the increased costs on to the end consumer depends on the regulators' flexibility. Concerning the global trade tensions, the negative consequences for the utility business couldn't be ruled out. The electrical transformers are among the list of most imported products in the U.S., with a significant portion of the equipment coming from Mexico and China.

Investment Conclusion

I will stick to the same model for the calculations of the XLU upside potential, which is based on the weighted average target prices of the fund's securities. The upside potential of the XLU should be 16%, which is almost twice that indicated in previous estimates.

We can spot that NextEra Energy, Constellation Energy, and Vista Corp. are deemed to contribute the largest upside potential among the fund holdings, or up to 9% combined. It's not surprising, since NEE benefits prominently from both stable cash flows from its regulated utility and the leadership position in the renewable energy business. CEG stands among the main beneficiaries from the clean energy transition, since the company operates the largest fleet of carbon-free energy facilities. Vistra Corp., in turn, could profit from its integrated business model and flexible energy solutions.

Looking at the earnings estimates, the utility sector is expected to register a 7.7% growth in the first quarter of 2025, which should strengthen the dividend generation capacity of the utility players. According to the DPS forecasts, the forward dividend yield of the ETF could be estimated at 3.4%. Along with the expected 100 bps interest rate cuts over the next 12-month horizon, this could be enough to close the yield gap to 10Y government bonds. As a result, I am upgrading the XLU potential from Hold to Buy, since the utility industry has a solid fundamental to benefit from the growth in energy demand. This could prove the XLU ETF as a tariff-resilient investment, which has the potential to deliver up to 20% total return over the forward 12-month horizon.
